title: Kibbeh
subtitle: Stuffed bulgur-and-lamb croquettes, deep-fried.
excerpt: Lemon-shaped croquettes of finely ground lamb and bulgur wheat, stuffed with a spiced lamb-and-pine-nut filling and deep-fried. The labour-intensive jewel of Lebanese and Syrian cookery, scored by the grandmothers who shape them perfectly.
sourceNotes: The Levantine kibbeh tradition codified across Lebanese, Syrian, and Palestinian regional cookery. The lemon shape and pine-nut filling are the classical Beirut household pattern.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the shaped uncooked kibbeh on a tray, then bag once solid. Fry from frozen, adding 2 minutes to the cooking time. Best within 2 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shell mixture and filling can both be made a day ahead. The shaped kibbeh can wait, refrigerated, for several hours before frying.

glossaryTerms.definition: The classical kibbeh is shaped like a small American football or rugby ball, pointed at both ends. A practised hand pinches a ball of dough around a finger to form a thin-walled shell, fills it, and tapers the ends closed. The thinner the shell, the more highly the cook is praised.

content.text: Kibbeh is a two-component dish. The shell is finely ground lamb pounded together with soaked bulgur and a quick spice mix until it forms a smooth, almost claylike dough. The filling is a separate mixture: more lamb fried with onion, allspice, cinnamon, and pine nuts, drained of its fat, then chilled. The shaping takes the most practice; the shell is pinched around a finger to form a hollow lemon, then a teaspoon of filling goes inside and the ends are tapered closed.

The first half-dozen will look misshapen. By the time you have made the last one, the rest will look like they came off a deli counter. This is a dish where practice produces visible improvement within one session.

items.prepNote: large, finely chopped, for the filling

content.text: A food processor for the shell mixture is non-optional unless you have a butcher who can twice-grind the meat to a paste. A deep stockpot for the oil. A bowl of cold water beside the worktop for keeping your hands wet during shaping.
Rinse {{bulgur-wheat}} of bulgur in cold water 3 times. Drain well. Cover with fresh cold water and leave 20 minutes. Drain again, squeezing out as much water as you can in a sieve.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Tip in the {{pine-nuts}} of pine nuts. Toss for 2 minutes until pale gold; lift out with a slotted spoon. Add the chopped {{onion}} of onion to the same pan and cook 8 minutes until soft. Tip in {{lamb-mince}} of lamb mince and break apart with a wooden spoon. Cook 8 minutes until any liquid has cooked away. Stir in {{allspice}} of allspice, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, the sumac if using, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Tip onto a plate to cool, then stir in the toasted pine nuts. Chill 30 minutes.

In the food processor, pulse the finely chopped onion with {{allspice}} of allspice, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper until very fine. Add {{lamb-mince}} of lamb mince and the drained bulgur. Add the {{ice}} of crushed ice. Process for 2 to 3 minutes, scraping down twice, until the mixture forms a smooth, claylike dough. Refrigerate 30 minutes; this firms the dough for shaping.

Wet your hands. Take a walnut-sized lump of shell mixture and roll it into a ball. Hold the ball in your non-dominant hand and press into the centre with the wet forefinger of your dominant hand, rotating the ball slowly so the cavity widens into a hollow shell. Aim for walls about 5 mm thick.

Drop a teaspoon of filling into the cavity. Pinch the opening closed, then taper the ends with wet fingers to form a lemon shape. Place on a lined tray. Continue until all the mixture is shaped, around 18 kibbeh.

Pour {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into the stockpot to a depth of 8 cm. Heat to 175°C. Lower 4 to 5 kibbeh in at a time with a slotted spoon. Fry for 4 to 5 minutes, turning halfway, until deep mahogany. Lift onto a rack. Repeat with the rest, letting the oil come back to temperature between batches. Eat hot with lemon wedges and a yoghurt-mint dip.

items.cause: Mixture too dry, or hands not wet enough.
items.fix: Dip hands in cold water every 2 to 3 kibbeh. If the mixture still cracks, work in a tablespoon of ice water through the processor.

items.cause: Walls too thin, or oil too hot.
items.fix: Aim for 5 mm walls — thinner is harder. Keep the oil at 175°C; above 180°C and the outside cooks before the inside heats through, building pressure.

items.cause: Filling not fully cooked before stuffing.
items.fix: Cook the filling fully before chilling. The shell needs only minutes of frying; the filling has to be ready to eat on contact.
Use a thermometer. Fry no more than 5 at a time, let the oil recover between batches.

items.cause: Coarse bulgur used instead of fine.
items.fix: Always use fine bulgur (#1) for kibbeh shells. Coarse bulgur is for tabbouleh and pilaf.

content.text: Kibbeh is the showpiece of Levantine cooking, with Beirut, Damascus, Aleppo, and Palestinian villages all claiming distinct regional versions. The raw kibbeh nayyeh of Lebanon, the baked kibbeh bil-saneeyeh of Syria, and the broth-poached kibbeh labanieh are siblings of this fried version. The dish is celebratory, the labour itself a marker of welcome.
